items.prompt: What total-cost expression compares a preprocessing strategy across q queries?

options.text: Preprocessing cost P(n) plus q times per-query cost Q(n).
Only Q(n).
Only P(n).
P(n)·q·Q(n).

feedback.reason: The full workload is P(n) + qQ(n).
feedback.details: Memory construction, updates, and output may add further terms.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_only: The query cost repeats q times and preprocessing also executes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.product_all: Sequential preprocessing and queries add rather than multiply together.

items.prompt: For one membership query on an unsorted array, why can sorting first be worse than a direct scan?

options.text: O(n log n) sorting plus O(log n) query exceeds one O(n) scan asymptotically.
Binary search is never correct.
A scan is O(1).
Library sorting has no cost.

feedback.reason: Fast query time can be overwhelmed by a one-time preprocessing cost when q is small.
feedback.details: The best strategy depends on the whole workload.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_wrong: It is correct after legal sorting.

items.prompt: When do prefix sums become attractive for range-sum queries on a static array?

options.text: When O(n) preprocessing can be shared across enough O(1) queries.
For every single query regardless of n.
When arbitrary updates occur after every query and the prefix array is never repaired.
Only when each query outputs the full array.

feedback.reason: Prefix sums trade one linear build for constant-time query evaluation.
feedback.details: The trade is strongest when data is static and q is large.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_query_always: A direct O(length) scan may be cheaper for one small query.

items.prompt: A hash set is built for repeated membership queries. Which cost is easy to omit?

options.text: O(n) build work and O(n) expected storage, in addition to expected O(1) queries.
The number of queries never matters.
Every membership query returns n values.
A hash set always requires sorting first.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing can reduce query time by spending time and memory up front.
feedback.details: A fair comparison includes all three resources.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_count_none: The benefit is tied to query volume.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting: Hashing does not require sorted input.

items.prompt: What happens to a precomputed index when the underlying data changes?

options.text: It may need incremental maintenance or rebuilding before future queries are correct.
Preprocessing remains correct automatically.
Updates always make queries faster.
Only output size changes.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing assumptions include data mutability.
feedback.details: Static-data query models cannot be transferred unchanged to update-heavy workloads.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.never_matters: The index may no longer match the data.

items.prompt: Which distinction matters between offline and online queries?

options.text: Offline processing may reorder or batch known queries, while online processing must answer before future queries are known.
There is no algorithmic difference.
Online means outputs must be sorted.
Offline preprocessing is free.

feedback.reason: The availability of future queries changes legal preprocessing and batching strategies.
feedback.details: The complexity explanation should state which workload model is assumed.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_always: Knowledge of the full workload can enable sorting or batching.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_sort_output: Online refers to arrival and response timing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.offline_no_cost: It still consumes time and memory.

items.prompt: Why can sorting as preprocessing be illegal even when it improves query time?

options.text: The original order or original indices may be part of the required result contract.
Sorting is illegal whenever it is O(n log n).
Sorting always removes duplicates.
Sorting is semantically illegal only when q = 1.

feedback.reason: A preprocessing transformation must preserve all required information or carry provenance.
feedback.details: Performance improvement cannot justify changing result meaning.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_slow_only: Cost does not determine semantic legality.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates: Sorting reorders but does not deduplicate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_query: Legality is independent of query count.

items.prompt: How should a break-even query count be derived?

options.text: Write total-cost expressions for both strategies and solve where one becomes smaller.
Use the word many without a formula.
Compare only per-query terms.
Choose the strategy whose query variable has the shorter name.

feedback.reason: A break-even point follows from P1 + qQ1 versus P2 + qQ2.
feedback.details: The resulting threshold may depend on n, constants, memory, and update frequency.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.guess_many: The threshold depends on n and both query costs.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_build: That removes the cost being amortized.

items.prompt: Which costs belong in a preprocessing-versus-query comparison? Select all that apply.

options.text: One-time build or sort cost.
Per-query cost multiplied by q.
Additional index or table storage.
Variable and function names.

feedback.reason: A complete comparison covers total time and storage.
feedback.details: Update maintenance and output can add further terms when present.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.memory: A faster strategy may exceed memory limits.

items.prompt: Which workload properties make preprocessing more attractive? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many queries reuse the same data.
The underlying data changes rarely or not at all.
The per-query improvement is substantial.
One tiny query with no future reuse.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing pays off through reuse and stability.
feedback.details: A one-shot workload often favors direct computation.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.large_query_saving: Each query recovers more of the initial cost.

items.prompt: Which facts can make an index unattractive despite fast queries? Select all that apply.

options.text: The index exceeds available memory.
Maintaining correctness after updates is expensive.
Too few queries occur to recover build cost.
The index answers queries quickly.

feedback.reason: Query speed is only one part of a data-structure tradeoff.
feedback.details: Build, maintenance, and memory can dominate.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_fast: That is the intended benefit, not a disadvantage.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.frequent_updates: Update cost becomes part of the workload.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.small_q: The amortized benefit is too small.

items.prompt: Which distinctions should be explicit in a repeated-query model? Select all that apply.

options.text: Static data versus data with updates.
Online arrival versus all queries known in advance.
One query versus many queries.
Whether loops are named outer and inner.

feedback.reason: The same asymptotic query primitive can lead to different total strategies under different workload contracts.
feedback.details: Stating the model prevents transferring an invalid tradeoff.

items.prompt: Arrange the comparison of a direct strategy and a preprocessed strategy.

elements.text: Write the direct total cost for q queries.
Write build cost plus q times indexed query cost.
Choose the strategy that satisfies the full workload with margin.

feedback.reason: A query-only comparison is incomplete.
feedback.details: The decision belongs to total workload, not one isolated operation.

items.prompt: Arrange the prefix-sum workflow for a static array.

elements.text: Build the prefix array in O(n).
Answer it with a constant number of prefix lookups and subtraction.
Reuse the same prefix array for remaining queries.

feedback.reason: The one-time build is recovered through repeated constant-time queries.
feedback.details: The model assumes updates do not invalidate the prefix data.

items.prompt: Arrange the review of a data structure under updates and queries.

elements.text: Derive the cost of each operation type.
Verify the maintained structure remains semantically correct after every update.

feedback.reason: Dynamic workloads need more than preprocessing and query terms.
feedback.details: A stale but fast index is not a valid solution.

items.prompt: An unsorted array of n values receives q membership queries. Strategy A scans in O(n) per query. Strategy B sorts once in O(n log n) and uses O(log n) binary search per query. Select both total-time expressions.

strategy_b: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

strategy_b.sort_plus_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: The direct scan repeats n work q times.
feedback.details: The indexed strategy pays sorting once and logarithmic work per query.

items.prompt: A static array of n values is preprocessed into prefix sums, then q range-sum queries are answered. Select total time and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: Each query is O(1), and the prefix array is not part of the returned output.

feedback.reason: The build scans n values and each of q queries uses constant work.
feedback.details: The prefix array is reusable auxiliary state.

items.prompt: A hash set is built from n values and answers q membership queries in expected O(1) each. Select expected total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Assume expected O(1) insertion and lookup and at most n distinct keys.

feedback.reason: Expected linear build plus expected constant-time queries gives O(n + q).
feedback.details: The index stores up to one entry per distinct input value.

items.prompt: For n = 1,000 and q = 1, compare q linear scans with sorting once plus q binary searches using log2 n ≈ 10. Select the dominant operation estimates.
constraints: Ignore constants and treat one comparison as one unit.

feedback.reason: The formulas expose a break-even near ten queries under the simplified model.
feedback.details: A real decision still considers constants, order legality, and memory.

items.prompt: A mutable dataset of n values receives u updates. After each update, a full O(n) prefix structure is rebuilt, and q queries cost O(1) each. Select total time.
constraints: The initial build cost is included in the same asymptotic term.

feedback.reason: Each of u updates triggers linear rebuilding, while queries are constant-time.
feedback.details: A static prefix-sum advantage can disappear under frequent updates.
